insights: Low-cost expenses (bottom tertile) take, on average, 10.96 days to process, while high-cost expenses (top tertile) take only 3.04 days — a 7.92-day absolute gap and a 3.6x relative difference in processing time.

The strong negative correlation (−0.7155) between expense amount and processing_time for processed items reveals a systemic inversion: higher financial stakes are associated with faster, not slower, processing — contradicting expectations of heightened scrutiny or complexity delays.

This inverse efficiency pattern is universal across all 7 departments, with Development showing the most severe disparity (Low: 13.4 days vs High: 2.14 days, 6.25x ratio), indicating structural prioritization bias rather than isolated departmental behavior.

Geographic analysis shows South America has the largest cost-tier processing gap (Low: 15.0 days vs High: 2.1 days, 7.14x ratio), signaling region-specific operational inequity that risks compliance exposure and employee dissatisfaction in high-latency locations.

The pattern persists consistently at the individual user level, confirming that the disparity is embedded in processing behavior — not attributable to outlier agents or random variation — and reflects a repeatable, system-level governance issue.

73.2% of expenses (366 of 500) have zero processing_time, meaning they remain unprocessed (Pending/Submitted/Declined), and the observed inverse relationship applies exclusively to the 134 Processed items — highlighting that processing decisions themselves are skewed by cost level.
summary: The analysis directly contradicts the intuitive assumption that higher-cost expenses require more time to process; instead, it reveals a consistent, statistically robust inverse relationship (r = −0.7155) where low-cost expenses suffer disproportionately long delays — averaging 10.96 days versus just 3.04 days for high-cost ones. This efficiency inequity is not isolated: it spans all departments (most extreme in Development), all regions (most severe in South America), and all individual users, confirming it is a systemic feature of the current processing workflow. The fact that 73.2% of expenses remain unprocessed further underscores that resource allocation and triage logic — likely favoring high-visibility or high-dollar items — actively deprioritizes lower-cost submissions, risking fairness, audit readiness, and employee trust. Next, focus should shift to auditing approval workflows, routing rules, and automation thresholds to identify where cost-based prioritization is explicitly or implicitly encoded.
